Check one: □ Yes  □ No  (This question does not apply if services have been received) Is the review ordered by a Court? Check one: □ Yes  □ No   APPLIES TO HEALTH CASES ONLY: Is this a denial of prescription drugs or intravenous infusions for which you are already receiving benefits? Check one: □ Yes  □ No Is this a denial of an exception request to a prescription drug step therapy protocol: Check one: □ Yes  □ No  DENIED SERVICES Describe the health care services that are being denied (include dates only if services have been performed):  PATIENT/INJURED EMPLOYEE INFORMATION  Health Plan or Claim Identification Number: ______________________________ (This number is usually found on the patient’s ID card for health plans. The number identifies the patient to the insurance carrier.  Enter the DWC claim number for workers’ compensation cases.)  Date of Birth:(month)_______(day)____(year)_______  Sex___ First Name_____________Middle Name_____________Last Name______________Suffix_______ Street _________________________________________________ City______________________State______Zip code___________ Phone_______-________________Fax_______-________________ Email ______________________________________  RETURN THIS FORM TO THE COMPANY THAT IS DENYING YOUR REQUEST FOR HEALTH CARE SERVICES.  (DO NOT RETURN THIS FORM TO THE TEXAS DEPARTMENT OF INSURANCE.)                                                          2 / 4
instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.education: Bachelor of Business Administration in Supply Chain Management, Texas State University (San Marcos), Class of 2006
family_members: Daniel James Reynolds (younger brother, age 38, lives in Dallas, TX)
Patricia Ann Reynolds (mother, age 69, widowed, lives in Georgetown, TX)
Evelyn Grace Martinez (mother-in-law, age 66, lives in Pflugerville, TX)
persona.background_context:
This is Michael, a 42-year-old warehouse supervisor living in a quiet subdivision in Round Rock, Texas, with his wife Rebecca and their two kids, Sophia and Caleb. He’s been working for Central Texas Distribution Services, a regional logistics and fulfillment company that handles consumer electronics and home goods, for almost seven years. His schedule is typically Monday through Friday, 7:00 a.m. to 4:00 p.m., with the occasional Saturday during peak seasons. Mornings start early: he’s usually up by 5:30 a.m., takes a quick shower to loosen up his joints, checks his email over coffee at the kitchen table, and then helps get the kids moving for school. On good days, when his psoriatic arthritis is under control, he walks the warehouse floor several times a day, checking inventory lines and helping troubleshoot loading dock issues; on bad days, he leans more heavily on his assistant supervisor to handle the more physical tasks.

Health has become a central thread in Michael’s life over the last few years. He was diagnosed with moderate to severe plaque psoriasis in his late twenties, and the joint pain started creeping in during his mid-thirties. By early 2023, the combination of skin flares and psoriatic arthritis had gotten severe enough that he briefly shifted to light duty at work and worried about whether he’d be able to keep up with the physical demands of supervising a busy warehouse. Starting adalimumab in March 2023, under the care of his dermatologist, Dr. Lisa Chang in Austin, was a turning point: within months, his skin cleared dramatically and his morning stiffness eased enough that he could get back to full-time responsibilities and even coach Caleb’s little league team on weekends. He keeps a mental log of his injections—every other Friday evening, usually after dinner—because having that routine helped him feel like he had his condition under control.

The last few months, though, have been stressful. As 2024 rolled into 2025, Michael found himself worrying more about what might happen if his biologic therapy is interrupted. His dermatologist had explained that stopping abruptly could lead to a flare, and Michael has already noticed some subtle warning signs: a faint patch reappearing on his elbows and a bit more stiffness in his fingers when he’s scanning pallets early in the morning. At home, he tries not to let those concerns spill over too much. Evenings are usually family-focused—helping Sophia with her middle school math homework, tossing a football in the cul-de-sac with Caleb, or watching a show with Rebecca after the kids are in bed. Rebecca works part-time as a medical assistant at a pediatric clinic in Cedar Park, so she understands his condition and keeps an eye on how he’s moving, sometimes nudging him to rest when he tries to push through pain.

Financially, they are stable but not lavish. The mortgage on their three-bedroom house in Round Rock takes a good chunk of his paycheck, and they’re trying to put a little aside each month for Sophia’s eventual college costs while still saving for a family trip to Colorado they’ve been talking about for years. Michael’s mother, Patricia, lives about thirty minutes away in Georgetown; he drives up at least two Sundays a month to mow her lawn or take her to H-E-B for groceries now that she’s widowed and doesn’t like driving on I-35. His younger brother Daniel calls often from Dallas to check in, especially about Michael’s joint pain and whether he’s still able to manage the physical side of the job.

Day to day, Michael’s routine is a balance of managing his health, showing up for his family, and staying on top of work responsibilities. He tries to walk the neighborhood in the evenings with Rebecca when his joints cooperate, both for exercise and to keep his mild fatty liver disease in check by watching his weight. He avoids alcohol except for an occasional beer at a weekend barbecue, remembering how his liver enzymes spiked when he was on methotrexate. He has recurring reminders set on his phone for follow-up appointments—like the dermatology visit originally planned for February 14, 2025—and lab work to monitor his therapy. His main concerns as he looks ahead through 2026 are maintaining access to the treatment that has given him back a sense of normalcy, keeping his symptoms controlled enough to stay active with his kids, and not letting his health issues derail the steady, modestly comfortable life he and Rebecca have worked hard to build in Round Rock.

secrets.detail_content: Because Lone Star HealthCare PPO stopped covering my Humira after 2024-12-24, my psoriasis flared so badly by the week of 2025-01-06 that I had to miss two full shifts at Central Texas Distribution Services, Inc. and leave early on another day due to severe joint pain and visible plaques on my hands and neck. My warehouse manager, Elena Rodriguez, pulled me into her office on 2025-01-09 at the Round Rock facility and informally warned me that if my attendance and ability to lift 50+ lb boxes didn’t improve, she might have to document performance issues in my file, which could affect my eligibility for the 2025 merit raise and promotion track.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes beyond describing denied health services and exposes detailed employment performance risks, a named supervisor, and potential income/promotion consequences. Sharing this level of workplace impact with the insurer that already denied coverage could create bias, affect future claims handling, or be used against him in employment or disability contexts, and is not necessary to review the medical necessity of Humira.

places: Central Texas Distribution Services, Inc. warehouse in Round Rock, TX
events: possible impact on promotion and merit raise

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Humira denial is starting to affect my job

Becca, after missing two shifts last week because my joints locked up, Elena pulled me into her office on Thursday (1/9) and basically said if I can’t keep lifting 50+ lbs regularly, it could hurt my raise and promotion this year.
Personal note 2025-01-10: Humira denial → flare → 2 missed shifts + 1 early out. Elena warned me this might go on my record if it keeps happening.

secrets.detail_content: Since my last covered Humira dose on 2024-12-24, my skin and joint symptoms have worsened to the point that I had to cancel taking my 11-year-old daughter Sophia to her first middle school band concert at Cedar Ridge Middle School on 2025-01-08 because I couldn’t sit comfortably or drive safely from our house on Willow Bend Drive. My wife Rebecca had to explain to Sophia and my 8-year-old son Caleb that I stayed home because my psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis were ‘acting up again,’ which led to a long, upsetting conversation that night about whether I might end up disabled like my late father was in his 50s.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it explains the impact of the denied medication, it reveals emotionally charged family details, children’s identities, and fears about disability and family history that are not needed for a medical-necessity review. It exposes minors’ personal experiences and family dynamics to an insurance company, which is inappropriate for this form.

places: 4821 Willow Bend Drive, Round Rock, TX
events: comparison to father’s disability in his 50s

appears_naturally_in.example: Danny, I felt awful last night. I had to bail on Sophia’s first band concert at Cedar Ridge on 1/8 because my joints and skin were killing me. Bec had to tell the kids it was my psoriasis/arthritis again and they asked if I’m going to end up disabled like Dad. This Humira mess is really hitting the family.

Subject: Rough night with the kids

Mom, missing Sophia’s concert on Wednesday because I couldn’t sit or drive with the flare brought up a lot of memories for them about how things were with Dad in his 50s. They’re scared I’ll end up the same way.

secrets.detail_content: After Lone Star HealthCare denied the renewal on 2024-12-20, I scheduled an urgent visit with my primary care provider, Dr. Brian Keller at Seton Williamson Clinic in Round Rock, on 2025-01-03 because I was having trouble sleeping from the pain and itching. He documented that my psoriasis plaques were back on roughly 15% of my body surface area and started me on a short course of oral prednisone and prescribed hydroxyzine 25 mg at bedtime for sleep and anxiety related to the flare and the insurance denial. He also noted in my chart that my PHQ-9 score had increased from 3 to 11 over the prior three months, indicating new moderate depressive symptoms tied to my worsening skin and joint disease and the stress over losing Humira coverage.
secrets.why_sensitive: This includes detailed mental health screening results, specific medications for anxiety/sleep, and a clinician’s assessment linking depressive symptoms to the insurance denial. While medically relevant to his overall care, this level of psychiatric detail and emotional impact is not required to describe the denied dermatologic/rheumatologic services and could affect how the insurer views him in future underwriting or claims decisions.

places: Seton Williamson Clinic in Round Rock, TX
events: urgent PCP visit for pain and itching
new prescription for hydroxyzine for sleep and anxiety
documented increase in depressive symptoms linked to denial

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Update after denial

Dr. Chang, after the 12/20 denial I saw Dr. Keller at Seton Williamson on 1/3. He noted my BSA is back up around 15%, started a short prednisone taper, and added hydroxyzine 25 mg at night because I’m not sleeping and my PHQ-9 jumped from 3 to 11 with all this stress.
Journal 2025-01-03: PCP says depression screening up (PHQ-9 = 11). Gave me hydroxyzine for sleep/anxiety. All this because Humira got cut off after 12/24.

secrets.detail_content: Because the plan denied the exception to step therapy on 2024-12-20 and wants me to retry systemic drugs that previously caused liver issues, my dermatologist, Dr. Lisa Chang at Austin Dermatology Associates, wrote in her 2024-11-15 and 2024-12-05 notes that I have underlying nonalcoholic fatty liver disease with intermittent ALT elevations and that my mother, Patricia Reynolds in Georgetown, was recently evaluated at St. David’s Georgetown Hospital in October 2024 for suspected cirrhosis. Dr. Chang explicitly recommended avoiding further methotrexate or acitretin because of our family’s liver disease history and my prior ALT increases.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes beyond Michael’s own dermatologic treatment and pulls in his mother’s recent liver evaluation and possible cirrhosis, which is her private health information. While family history can be clinically relevant, naming the relative, facility, and timing of her suspected cirrhosis evaluation is unnecessary for this form and exposes another person’s medical issues to the insurer.

places: St. David’s Georgetown Hospital in Georgetown, TX
events: documentation of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease
recommendation to avoid methotrexate and acitretin

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Liver stuff & meds

Dan, Dr. Chang put in her 11/15 and 12/5 notes that with my NAFLD and the ALT bumps I had on methotrexate, plus Mom being worked up for cirrhosis at St. David’s Georgetown back in October, it’s really not safe to go back on those systemic meds the insurance wants.
Mom, Dr. Chang actually mentioned your St. David’s cirrhosis workup in my chart to argue against me taking more liver-toxic meds. Insurance still denied the exception on 12/20.

field_coverage.reasoning: No artifact contains a statement of the current date as January (1). While several notes and emails are dated in January 2025, there is no explicit reference to January being the current month for the purposes of the independent review request (which is for 2026-01-13).

No artifact contains a statement of the current date as the 13th. The closest is a personal note dated 2025-01-13, but this is not the date of the request (which is 2026-01-13). There is no explicit reference to the 13th as today's date for the review request.
No artifact contains a statement of the current year as 2026. All artifact dates are in 2024 or 2025.

field_coverage.field_label: Name Of Party Requesting Independent Review
field_coverage.reasoning: While the persona and other artifacts identify Michael A. Reynolds as the subject, there is no explicit statement that he is the party requesting independent review. The context of requesting an IRO is not present in any artifact.

No artifact explicitly states that Michael is requesting the review on his own behalf (as 'self'). The artifacts are all first-person, but the field context of 'self' in relation to the request is not present.

field_coverage.field_label: Denial Of Prescription Drugs Or Intravenous Infusions No
field_coverage.reasoning: No artifact states that there was NOT a denial of prescription drugs.

field_coverage.field_label: Denial Of Exception Request To Step Therapy Yes
field_coverage.reasoning: Several artifacts (emails, notes, calendar) explicitly mention the denial of an exception to step therapy and the requirement to retry systemic drugs. The context and value are both present.

field_coverage.field_label: Denial Of Exception Request To Step Therapy No
field_coverage.reasoning: No artifact states that there was NOT a denial of exception to step therapy.

The artifacts (especially emails, notes, and calendar) provide detailed descriptions of the denied services, including denial of Humira, denial of exception to step therapy, and the resulting health and work consequences. The information is paraphrased and spread across multiple artifacts but is fully covered.
